content: KNEE REPLACEMENT SURGERY - POST-OPERATIVE CARE GUIDELINES. Total knee replacement (TKR) is a common orthopedic procedure requiring comprehensive post-operative care. This guideline covers essential recovery aspects including pain management, wound care, physical therapy, and activity progression. SWELLING MANAGEMENT. How long does swelling last after knee replacement surgery? Swelling is a normal part of healing. Patients should expect: Weeks 1-2: Significant swelling and bruising around the knee and lower leg. Elevation above heart level is crucial. Weeks 3-6: Swelling gradually decreases but may fluctuate with activity levels. Many patients notice increased swelling by evening after being active during the day. Months 3-6: Mild to moderate swelling persists intermittently, especially after prolonged standing or walking. 6+ Months: Most patients experience minimal swelling by 6 months, though some may have occasional mild puffiness up to one year post-surgery. Management Strategies: Elevate the leg above heart level for 30-45 minutes, 3-4 times daily. Apply ice packs wrapped in a thin towel for 15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours. Wear compression stockings as prescribed. Avoid sitting with legs dependent for extended periods. Perform ankle pumps regularly to promote circulation. PAIN MANAGEMENT AND MEDICATIONS. What painkillers are safe to take after knee surgery? Effective pain management is essential for recovery and participation in physical therapy. Immediate Post-Operative (Weeks 1-2): Opioids: Oxycodone, hydrocodone, or tramadol as prescribed. Use the lowest effective dose and taper as soon as possible. NSAIDs: Ibuprofen (400-600mg every 6-8 hours) or naproxen (220-500mg twice daily) to reduce inflammation. Acetaminophen: 500-1000mg every 6 hours for baseline pain control (maximum 3000-4000mg daily depending on liver function). Subacute Phase (Weeks 3-6): Transition to non-opioid medications as pain decreases. Continue NSAIDs for inflammation management. Consider topical analgesics (diclofenac gel, lidocaine patches). Long-term Management (6+ weeks): Acetaminophen as needed for mild discomfort. NSAIDs only if approved by surgeon (may interfere with bone healing). Non-pharmacological methods: ice, elevation, rest periods. MOBILITY AND ACTIVITY PROGRESSION. When can I start walking without crutches after knee replacement? Week 1-2: Weight-bearing as tolerated with walker or crutches. Focus on heel-to-toe gait pattern. Short distances only (household ambulation). Weeks 3-4: Transition to single crutch or cane as strength and confidence improve. Most patients can ambulate without assistive devices indoors by week 4-6. Weeks 6-8: Most patients walk independently without assistive devices. May still use cane for long distances or fatigue management. Gait should appear relatively normal without significant limp. Weeks 8-12: Full independent ambulation expected. Return to normal daily activities without assistive devices. INFECTION PREVENTION AND RECOGNITION. Signs of infection after knee surgery. Infection is a serious complication requiring immediate attention. Patients must monitor for: Early Warning Signs (Contact surgeon immediately): Temperature greater than 101°F (38.3°C). Increasing redness, warmth, or swelling around the incision. Wound drainage that is cloudy, yellow, green, or foul-smelling. Red streaks extending from the incision site. Incision opening or separation. Systemic Symptoms (Seek emergency care): Chills and shaking, confusion or altered mental status. Rapid heart rate or breathing difficulty. Severe or worsening pain unrelieved by medication. Wound Care Instructions: Keep incision dry for 48-72 hours post-surgery. Once cleared by surgeon, gentle washing with mild soap and water. Pat dry completely - do not rub. No soaking (baths, hot tubs, swimming) until incision fully healed (typically 4-6 weeks). PHYSICAL THERAPY AND EXERCISE PROTOCOL. Physical therapy exercises for knee replacement recovery. Phase 1: Immediate Post-Operative (Days 1-14). Ankle Pumps: Flex and point ankles 10-15 times every hour while awake. Promotes circulation and prevents blood clots. Quad Sets: Tighten thigh muscle, pressing knee down into bed. Hold 5 seconds, repeat 10-15 times, 3-4 times daily. Heel Slides: Slide heel toward buttocks, bending knee as much as possible. Hold 5 seconds, slowly straighten. 10 repetitions, 3 times daily. Straight Leg Raises: Tighten quadriceps, lift straight leg 6-12 inches. Hold 5 seconds, lower slowly. Progress to 3 sets of 10-15 repetitions. Phase 2: Early Rehabilitation (Weeks 2-6). Active range of motion exercises progressing to 90+ degrees flexion. Stationary cycling with minimal resistance when ROM allows. Terminal knee extension: Roll towel under ankle, allow knee to straighten fully. Hold 5-10 minutes. Weight-shifting and balance exercises standing at counter. Expected Milestones: 90 degrees flexion by 2 weeks. 110 degrees flexion by 6 weeks. Straight (0 degrees) extension by 6 weeks. Normal or near-normal gait by 8-12 weeks. BLOOD CLOT PREVENTION. Blood clot prevention after surgery. Blood clots (deep vein thrombosis - DVT) are a significant risk after knee replacement. Pharmacological Prevention: Blood thinners (anticoagulants) prescribed for 2-4 weeks post-surgery. Options include aspirin, low molecular weight heparin, warfarin, or direct oral anticoagulants. Mechanical Prevention: Compression stockings (TED hose) worn during day for 2-6 weeks. Sequential compression devices (SCDs) used in hospital and potentially at home. Ankle pumps every hour while awake. Activity-Based Prevention: Early and frequent walking (primary prevention method). Avoid prolonged sitting or lying in one position. Change position every 1-2 hours. Leg elevation when resting. DVT Warning Signs: Sudden calf pain or tenderness. Unilateral leg swelling or warmth. Chest pain, shortness of breath (possible pulmonary embolism - EMERGENCY).
